subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Master's degree
Master's degree is in Marketing, Business, or a related field supporting research and analytics
Completed undergraduate studies in communications or business prior to graduate degree
Has attended multiple professional development workshops on digital marketing and analytics
Possesses certification in Google Analytics and other industry-standard marketing tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Search Marketing Strategist
Role focuses on researching, analyzing, and developing marketing strategies based on data
Acts as a key researcher and strategist within the marketing team
Responsible for preparing reports and recommendations for organizational decision-makers

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the digital marketing and research sector
Specializes in search marketing and data-driven strategy
Engages with technology and SaaS platforms regularly as part of research

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed at a mid-sized company (between 50-200 employees)
Company culture emphasizes collaboration and evidence-based decision-making
Remote and hybrid work options available, with reliance on online collaboration tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Works for a company serving B2B clients in the marketing and analytics domain
Focuses on providing research and strategic recommendations for digital marketing initiatives
Clients span multiple industries but share a need for data-backed marketing solutions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has over 10 years of experience in search marketing and research strategy
Current role has been held for at least 5 years within the current organization
Previously held similar roles at other companies prior to current position

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds a senior-level position within the organization
Frequently leads initiatives and mentors junior colleagues
Considered a subject matter expert in search marketing research

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Performs extensive research on market trends, competitors, and keywords
Analyzes collected data using spreadsheets and analytics tools
Develops and documents data-driven marketing strategies
Prepares detailed reports and presentations for management
Presents findings and recommendations to superiors for decision-making

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Researching and data collection: approximately 50% of weekly workload
Data analysis and organization: about 30% of time
Strategy development, report writing, and presentations: around 20% of time
Occasional collaboration and meetings with team members as needed

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Exercises significant autonomy in research design and data analysis
Authority to recommend strategies but final decisions are made by superiors
Responsible for independently identifying relevant information and insights
Expected to justify recommendations with thorough data and analysis

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Researching information online using advanced search queries
Organizing and cleaning data in spreadsheets
Synthesizing findings into clear, actionable summaries
Creating structured reports based on defined templates

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Interpreting ambiguous or incomplete data sets
Identifying actionable insights from large, unstructured data sources
Developing strategies for new or rapidly changing market segments
Ensuring that reports anticipate potential objections from decision-makers

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Collecting large volumes of keyword or competitor data from multiple sources
Reformatting and standardizing data for analysis
Preparing initial drafts of routine market research reports
Tracking and recording ongoing campaign metrics in spreadsheets

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys analyzing data to uncover unique trends or patterns
Finds satisfaction in developing innovative marketing strategies
Considers repetitive data entry and formatting to be tedious
Enjoys presenting findings and discussing implications with colleagues

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual data collection from multiple online sources can be time-consuming
Data cleaning and deduplication often slow down the analysis process
Occasional difficulties integrating findings from diverse data formats
Limited automation for recurring reporting tasks increases workload

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Finds that high skill level in research and analysis leads to greater enjoyment and efficiency
Enjoyment increases when tasks involve creative problem-solving or novel challenges
Productivity is highest when using familiar tools and well-defined processes
Less enjoyable tasks tend to be more repetitive and less intellectually stimulating

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Open and proactive about adopting new technologies that improve workflow
Views tech learning as essential to staying relevant in the marketing field
Enjoys experimenting with new software if it promises clear time savings

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Considers self adaptable, having transitioned through several tool upgrades over career
Quickly learns new platforms, especially when related to data analysis or research
Comfortable using early-access or beta versions of industry tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to devote several hours per month to learning new tools if they add value
Prefers self-paced online courses and webinars for ongoing training
Has a track record of completing certification programs in Google Analytics and related areas

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by potential for increased efficiency and better results
Barriers include occasional high workload and competing project deadlines
Prefers learning technologies directly applicable to daily work over more experimental tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers often share tips about new tools and best practices, encouraging adoption
Management supports tech upskilling and sometimes mandates tool transitions
Team culture values innovation and rewards early adoption of productivity-enhancing software

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Google Workspace (Docs, Sheets) daily for documentation and data management
Relies on Microsoft Excel for advanced data analysis
Conducts online research using Google Search and industry databases
Communicates via Slack and Zoom for team collaboration

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Spreadsheets (Google Sheets, Excel) are vital for organizing and analyzing data
Google Docs is used for drafting and editing reports
Trello is occasionally used for managing tasks and project timelines
Canva is used for creating visual elements in presentations and reports

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient with Google Analytics and other SEO tracking tools
Experienced with keyword research tools such as SEMrush or Ahrefs
Familiar with collaborative platforms like Slack, Zoom, and Trello
Keeps up to date with new features and updates in core industry software

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally experiences challenges transferring data between Excel and Google Sheets
Manual exports/imports needed when integrating data from various research tools
Some visualization tools require data reformatting before use
Lack of seamless integration between Trello and other reporting tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands concepts like natural language processing, predictive analytics, and machine learning
Can distinguish between generative AI tools (like LLMs) and traditional automation
Knowledgeable about the applications of AI in data analysis and report generation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses large language models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT or Gemini for summarization and drafting
Has experimented with AI-enhanced research tools like MarketMuse or Clearscope
Employs Google Workspace's built-in AI features for smart suggestions and data insights

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs weekly for summarizing research findings and drafting report sections
Applies AI tools for automating repetitive data collection and initial analysis
Occasionally uses AI to brainstorm strategy ideas or generate keyword lists

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used LLMs to speed up initial report drafting, reducing turnaround by 30%
Encountered inaccuracies in AI-generated summaries, requiring careful fact-checking
Learned to combine AI outputs with domain knowledge for best results
Noted that AI tools sometimes miss context-specific nuances in marketing data

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Receives informal AI tool tips from colleagues via Slack channels
Access to periodic internal webinars on best practices for AI in research
No formalized, company-wide AI training program currently in place

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI tools to maintain control over outputs
Often iterates on AI-generated drafts rather than accepting first results
Uses AI as a brainstorming assistant rather than a fully autonomous agent

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI as an assistant for research and analysis tasks
Leverages AI for idea generation and summarization, then adds expert review
Uses AI to handle routine tasks, freeing up time for higher-level strategy work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to integrating AI into existing workflows if it demonstrably improves productivity
Willing to pilot new AI features and provide feedback to developers
Prefers gradual adoption, ensuring processes remain transparent and controllable

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational AI interfaces for brainstorming and summarization
Finds command-based interfaces efficient for structured data tasks
Uses both types depending on the complexity and context of the task

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many tasks require deep understanding of search marketing trends and context
Relies on domain knowledge to interpret ambiguous data and guide strategy
Uses tacit expertise to validate or adjust AI-generated recommendations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for routine data organization and initial drafting
Exercises caution with AI-generated insights, always verifying against raw data
Does not rely solely on AI for critical decisions or nuanced strategy recommendations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to automate repetitive data collection and formatting
Believes human expertise should drive final analysis and strategic decisions
Supports a hybrid approach where AI accelerates workflow without replacing expert judgment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: High automation acceptable for standardized, low-risk tasks such as data cleaning
Resistant to automation in areas requiring nuanced judgment or creative problem-solving
Feels threatened by automation that lacks transparency or interpretability

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the value of analytical and interpretive skills
Routine technical skills (e.g., data entry) becoming less central due to automation
Emphasizes the increasing importance of strategic thinking and domain expertise

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing prompt engineering skills to get better results from LLMs
Learning to validate and correct AI outputs as part of quality control
Gaining experience in integrating AI tools into workflow automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Some concern about AI replacing repetitive data tasks, but not core strategic responsibilities
Confident that human insight remains indispensable for high-level marketing decisions
Stays updated on AI trends to ensure continued relevance and value in the role

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Manually reviews and edits all AI-generated summaries and reports
Ensures ability to revert to human-only processes if AI output is unsatisfactory
Company policy requires human sign-off on all critical deliverables involving AI

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company increasingly encourages responsible AI adoption for productivity gains
Policies evolving to include more guidance on AI transparency and accountability
Team discussions on ethical AI use have become more frequent in recent years

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will significantly improve efficiency and productivity in marketing research
Sees potential for AI to democratize access to high-quality analysis across organizations
Recognizes risks of bias and misinformation if AI is misapplied

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports transparent, explainable AI systems in professional settings
Concerned about data privacy and potential for algorithmic bias
Advocates for human oversight in any AI-driven decision-making process

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would like AI tools that better understand industry-specific context and jargon
Desires seamless integration between AI and existing research/reporting platforms
Sees value in AI tools capable of more advanced data visualization and storytelling

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects more time will be devoted to interpreting AI-generated insights and less to manual data tasks
Foresees a shift toward orchestrating and validating AI-driven research pipelines
Believes strategic thinking and cross-functional collaboration will become even more critical

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Implement regular training and upskilling sessions focused on responsible AI use
Develop clear policies for human oversight and accountability in AI-driven processes
Invest in AI tool integrations that align with existing workflows and business goals
